## WorkRetired                        0.5030868  2.1874593   0.230  0.81816    
## WorkSchool                         7.7889612  6.4313348   1.211  0.22624    
## Age                                0.0135097  0.0044665   3.025  0.00257 ** 
## Educ                               0.0825263  0.0206934   3.988 7.31e-05 ***
## Hours                              0.0010146  0.0043255   0.235  0.81461    
## log(TVHours + 1)                  -0.1947840  0.1094176  -1.780  0.07545 .  
## MEduc                             -0.0082997  0.0191054  -0.434  0.66411    
## FEduc                              0.0281617  0.0165624   1.700  0.08948 .  
## log(Sibs + 1)                      0.1216572  0.0900660   1.351  0.17718    
## WorkHousekeeping:Age               0.0007984  0.0126450   0.063  0.94967    
## WorkLaid Off:Age                   0.0370831  0.0910906   0.407  0.68405    
## WorkNot Working:Age               -0.1128065  0.1167466  -0.966  0.33423    
## WorkOther:Age                      0.0135811  0.0766288   0.177  0.85937    
## WorkParttime:Age                   0.0102467  0.0101200   1.013  0.31162    
## WorkRetired:Age                   -0.0254000  0.0192396  -1.320  0.18717    
## WorkSchool:Age                     0.0025342  0.0392249   0.065  0.94850    
## WorkHousekeeping:Educ             -0.0427066  0.0686667  -0.622  0.53417    
## WorkLaid Off:Educ                 -0.0993491  0.6328440  -0.157  0.87530    
## WorkNot Working:Educ               0.5730530  0.4482570   1.278  0.20150    
## WorkOther:Educ                     0.3171202  0.5450908   0.582  0.56089    
## WorkParttime:Educ                 -0.1338918  0.0556635  -2.405  0.01640 *  
## WorkRetired:Educ                   0.0776308  0.0864866   0.898  0.36968    
## WorkSchool:Educ                   -0.2931145  0.1824101  -1.607  0.10850    
## WorkHousekeeping:Hours             0.0119033  0.0110098   1.081  0.27997    
## WorkLaid Off:Hours                -0.0279512  0.0752871  -0.371  0.71055    
## WorkNot Working:Hours              0.0040528  0.0253795   0.160  0.87317    
## WorkOther:Hours                    0.0144432  0.0995840   0.145  0.88472    
## WorkParttime:Hours                -0.0064388  0.0105632  -0.610  0.54235    
## WorkRetired:Hours                 -0.0008699  0.0120310  -0.072  0.94238    
## WorkSchool:Hours                   0.0086353  0.0187365   0.461  0.64502    
## WorkHousekeeping:log(TVHours + 1)  0.4225319  0.3085298   1.370  0.17125    
## WorkLaid Off:log(TVHours + 1)      1.0348426  1.0411201   0.994  0.32056    
## WorkNot Working:log(TVHours + 1)   1.6886198  1.5992931   1.056  0.29137    
## WorkOther:log(TVHours + 1)         0.6526769  1.7689673   0.369  0.71226    
## WorkParttime:log(TVHours + 1)     -0.7357416  0.2812336  -2.616  0.00907 ** 
## WorkRetired:log(TVHours + 1)       0.1809156  0.4012588   0.451  0.65221    
## WorkSchool:log(TVHours + 1)       -0.9726081  0.6445197  -1.509  0.13171    
## WorkHousekeeping:MEduc             0.0063575  0.0725264   0.088  0.93017    
## WorkLaid Off:MEduc                -0.1799601  0.2625496  -0.685  0.49328    
## WorkNot Working:MEduc             -0.1407431  0.2343068  -0.601  0.54823    
## WorkOther:MEduc                   -0.0038677  0.3333820  -0.012  0.99075    
## WorkParttime:MEduc                 0.0048837  0.0493252   0.099  0.92116    
## WorkRetired:MEduc                 -0.0013848  0.0593096  -0.023  0.98138    
## WorkSchool:MEduc                  -0.0460319  0.3050920  -0.151  0.88011    
## WorkHousekeeping:FEduc             0.0536626  0.0617010   0.870  0.38473    
## WorkLaid Off:FEduc                -0.0486919  0.2600907  -0.187  0.85155    
## WorkNot Working:FEduc             -0.1823208  0.2305689  -0.791  0.42934    
## WorkOther:FEduc                           NA         NA      NA       NA    
## WorkParttime:FEduc                -0.0232042  0.0416333  -0.557  0.57746    
## WorkRetired:FEduc                 -0.0485290  0.0566812  -0.856  0.39217    
## WorkSchool:FEduc                  -0.2347872  0.2282919  -1.028  0.30407    
## WorkHousekeeping:log(Sibs + 1)     0.0083198  0.2918398   0.029  0.97726    
## WorkLaid Off:log(Sibs + 1)        -0.6295618  1.4804409  -0.425  0.67077    
## WorkNot Working:log(Sibs + 1)      0.6286333  0.7795345   0.806  0.42025    
## WorkOther:log(Sibs + 1)                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## WorkParttime:log(Sibs + 1)        -0.0605811  0.2444690  -0.248  0.80435    
## WorkRetired:log(Sibs + 1)         -0.2720402  0.2997300  -0.908  0.36437    
## WorkSchool:log(Sibs + 1)           0.0332938  0.5440693   0.061  0.95122    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.119 on 753 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1437, Adjusted R-squared:  0.07435 
## F-statistic: 2.072 on 61 and 753 DF,  p-value: 7.01e-06

Work has 8 levels, so we have 7 dummy variables times our have 7 quantitative variables, so 49 interaction terms. We want to reduce our model to avoid over fitting. We perform backward elimination since we already have our linear model with all possible covariates and interactions.

ii) Formulate a suitable final model to draw inferences on the response variable and provide the interpretation of each type of coefficient in your final model.

We are using a backward elimination technique where we remove the lowest p-value per the ANOVA test, since we have quantitative and qualitative covariates.

# anova(full_int)

full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:MEduc)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:log(Sibs + 1))
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - MEduc)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:Hours)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:FEduc)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:Age)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Hours)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - FEduc)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:Educ)
# anova(full_int)
full_int = update(full_int, . ~ . - Work:log(TVHours + 1))
anova(full_int)
summary(full_int)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = log(Income) ~ Work + Age + Educ + log(TVHours + 
##     1) + log(Sibs + 1), data = gss)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.8316 -0.5004  0.0504  0.4226  6.7003 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)       9.241059   0.313054  29.519  < 2e-16 ***
## WorkHousekeeping -0.530434   0.158620  -3.344 0.000864 ***
## WorkLaid Off      0.039932   0.373731   0.107 0.914937    
## WorkNot Working  -0.541399   0.312714  -1.731 0.083784 .  
## WorkOther         0.077172   0.459982   0.168 0.866805    
## WorkParttime     -0.139585   0.121542  -1.148 0.251124    
## WorkRetired      -0.892310   0.201476  -4.429 1.08e-05 ***
## WorkSchool       -0.439255   0.316057  -1.390 0.164975    
## Age               0.011528   0.003294   3.499 0.000492 ***
## Educ              0.082639   0.015980   5.171 2.94e-07 ***
## log(TVHours + 1) -0.225616   0.085992  -2.624 0.008863 ** 
## log(Sibs + 1)     0.072470   0.070781   1.024 0.306204    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 1.112 on 803 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.09851,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.08616 
## F-statistic: 7.977 on 11 and 803 DF,  p-value: 2.785e-13

After applying a backwards elimination technique on the transformed model, we get 12 predictors (including the intercept) as the lowest and best options for future predicitions.

In the prediction error graph there are some incredible spikes. This is likely caused by multicollinearity within the 49 interaction terms. It is also possible that it was caused by using a 60/20/20 split, which only trains on 489 so the laid off or other categories in work may be under utilized (only having a small amount of n dedicated to it). The spike around 40 specifically is likely caused because we removed a variable that was shielding against collinearity causing the error to spike, but it still had to get removed since it was insignificant to the model and did not minimize the Mean Squared Predicition Error.

The MSPE resides at 1.07 which is relatively low, given our outliers and leverage points, so this model is so far a significant improvement than what we had prior; thanks to the transformations and the backwards elimination.

Step 9:

Ignore all interaction effects from now on.

  1. Fit a mixed-effects model with all available quantitative and qualitative covariates by appropriately treating some of your covariates as random effects. Determine a suitable transformation for your response variable.

  2. Test the statistical significance of all your fixed effects.

  3. Calculate the intraclass correlation coefficients corresponding to each of your random effects What percentage of the total variation in your response variable does each of your random effects explain?

  4. What’s another interpretation for each intraclass correlation coefficient?

i) Ignore all interaction effects from now on. Fit a mixed-effects model with all available quantitative and qualitative covariates by appropriately treating some of your covariates as random effects. Determine a suitable transformation for your response variable.

library(lme4)

mixed_model = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    Happy + MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 |
    Marital) + (1 | Work), data = gss)

sigma = data.frame(summary(mixed_model)$varcor)$sdcor
names(sigma) = data.frame(summary(mixed_model)$varcor)$grp
ICC = sigma^2/sum(sigma^2)
print(ICC)
##      Zodiac        Work     Marital    Residual 
## 0.003463625 0.053966054 0.055065667 0.887504654
summary(mixed_model, correlation = FALSE)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML ['lmerMod']
## Formula: log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +  
##     MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 |  
##     Marital) + (1 | Work)
##    Data: gss
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 2489.2
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.2700 -0.4054 -0.0073  0.3117  5.9940 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups   Name        Variance Std.Dev.
##  Zodiac   (Intercept) 0.004464 0.06681 
##  Work     (Intercept) 0.069556 0.26373 
##  Marital  (Intercept) 0.070973 0.26641 
##  Residual             1.143890 1.06953 
## Number of obs: 815, groups:  Zodiac, 12; Work, 8; Marital, 5
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value
## (Intercept)        8.2261484  0.4133664  19.900
## Age                0.0090210  0.0034370   2.625
## Educ               0.0774983  0.0166455   4.656
## Hours              0.0003294  0.0031349   0.105
## log(TVHours + 1)  -0.1774335  0.0839616  -2.113
## HappyPretty Happy  0.3758767  0.1381257   2.721
## HappyVery Happy    0.5157427  0.1477088   3.492
## MEduc             -0.0105824  0.0150634  -0.703
## FEduc              0.0231915  0.0131725   1.761
## log(Sibs + 1)      0.1351602  0.0706531   1.913
## SexMale            0.1229452  0.0791305   1.554
Y = gss$Income
alpha = 0.05
lambda = seq(-2, 2, 0.01)
loglik = numeric(length(lambda))
for (i in 1:length(lambda)) {
    if (lambda[i] == 0) {
        Ypower = log(Y)
    } else {
        Ypower = (Y^lambda[i] - 1)/lambda[i]
    }
    power = lmer(Ypower ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
        MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
        (1 | Work), data = gss)

    loglik[i] = logLik(power)[1] + (lambda[i] - 1) * sum(log(Y))
}
CI = range(lambda[loglik > max(loglik) - qchisq(alpha, 1, lower.tail = FALSE)/2])
print(CI)
## [1] -0.13 -0.09
plot(lambda, loglik, "l", xlab = expression(lambda), ylab = "Profile Log-Likelihood",
    lwd = 2)
abline(h = max(loglik) - qchisq(alpha, 1, lower.tail = FALSE)/2, lty = 2,
    lwd = 2)
abline(v = CI, lty = 2, lwd = 2)

If 0 is inside the CI then a log transformation is used, but the CI is [-0.13 -0.09] which does not include 0 but is very close. Which also backsup our previous transformation that we did prior, showing that a log transformation IS a suitable transformation even for this mixed effects model. The residuals show a range from \(-4.27\) to \(5.99\); the median is very close to zero (\(-0.0073\)) wihch suggests that the log transformation has done a good job of stabilizing the variance. So, we conclude that log(Income) is suitable even for this mixed effects model.

Since our response variable Income was was very skewed and needs a transformation and the optimal lambda is close to 0, a log transformation is reasonable. We confirm that

ii) Test the statistical significance of all your fixed effects.

Statistical Significance Testing for Age:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.2997707

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of Age on log(Income) is equal to 0.2997707.

Statistical Significance Testing for Educ:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
## Warning in checkConv(attr(opt, "derivs"), opt$par, ctrl = control$checkConv, : Model failed to converge with max|grad| = 0.00343929 (tol = 0.002, component 1)
##   See ?lme4::convergence and ?lme4::troubleshooting.
LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.000610675

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of Educ on log(Income) is equal to 0.000610675.

Statistical Significance Testing for Hours:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.9999998

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of Hours on log(Income) is equal to 0.9999998

Statistical Significance Testing for log(TVHours + 1):

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + Happy + MEduc + FEduc +
    log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) + (1 | Work),
    data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.4771

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of log(TVHours + 1) on log(Income) is equal to 0.4771

Statistical Significance Testing for Happy:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.0296004

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of Happy on log(Income) is equal to 0.0296004.

Statistical Significance Testing for MEduc:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    Happy + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.9921011

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of MEduc on log(Income) is equal to 0.9921011

Statistical Significance Testing for FEduc:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    Happy + MEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.6757152

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of FEduc on log(Income) is equal to 0.6757152

Statistical Significance Testing for log(Sibs + 1):

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    Happy + MEduc + FEduc + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) + (1 |
    Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.5954642

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of log(Sibs + 1) on log(Income) is equal to 0.5954642

Statistical Significance Testing for Sex:

full = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) + Happy +
    MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + Sex + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)
reduced = lmer(log(Income) ~ Age + Educ + Hours + log(TVHours + 1) +
    Happy + MEduc + FEduc + log(Sibs + 1) + (1 | Zodiac) + (1 | Marital) +
    (1 | Work), data = gss, REML = FALSE)

LR = -2 * (logLik(reduced)[1] - logLik(full)[1])
pvalue = pchisq(LR, 5, lower.tail = FALSE)
print(pvalue)
## [1] 0.7789858

The p-value of the Likelihood Ratio test for the effect of Sex on log(Income) is equal to 0.7789858

Out of every fixed effect covariate, the only statistically significant ones were Educ (p-value of 0.000610675) and Happy (p-value of 0.0296004). Every other fixed effect covariate remained statistically insignificant as their p-values through the Likelihood Ratio test were above the alpha threshold of 0.05.

iii) Calculate the intraclass correlation coefficients corresponding to each of your random effects. What percentage of the total variation in your response variable does each of your random effects explain?

Zodiac Intraclass Correlation Coefficient:

ICC_Zodiac = (0.004464/(0.004464 + 0.069556 + 0.070973 + 1.14389))
ICC_Zodiac
## [1] 0.003463464

Zodiac explains roughly \(0.35\%\) of the total variation in log(Income), which is extremely, extremely small. It has very low similarities between observations within the same group. TTwo people with the same Zodiac status, the predicted correlation between their log-incomes is \(0.0034\).

Work Intraclass Correlation Coefficient:

ICC_Sibs = (0.069556/(0.004464 + 0.069556 + 0.070973 + 1.14389))
ICC_Sibs
## [1] 0.05396611

Work explains roughly \(5.4\%\) of the total variation in log(Income), which is pretty small. It has low similarities between observations within the same group. Two people with the same Work status, the predicted correlation between their log-incomes is \(0.054\).

Marital Intraclass Correlation Coefficient:

ICC_Marital = (0.070973/(0.004464 + 0.069556 + 0.070973 + 1.14389))
ICC_Marital
## [1] 0.05506551

Marital explains roughly \(5.5\%\) of the total variation in log(Income), which is pretty small. It has low similarities between observations within the same group. Two people with the same marital status, the predicted correlation between their log-incomes is \(0.055\).

Residual Intraclass Correlation Coefficient:

ICC_Marital = (1.14389/(0.004464 + 0.069556 + 0.070973 + 1.14389))
ICC_Marital
## [1] 0.8875049

The residuals explain \(88.75\%\) of the total varition in log(Income), this means most of the total variation in log(Income) can not be explained by our random variables. Which is not optimal for our linear model, since we would rather have a higher degree of total variability in log(Income) be explained by our covaraites rather than just residuals.

iv) What’s another interpretation for each intraclass correlation coefficient?

The ICC represents the expected correlation between log(Income) of two different individuals who belong to the same group, examples stated above in section iii just above. ICC is a measure of within group similarity. A higher ICC means that individuals within the same category are more alike regarding their income than individuals chosen from the population at large. And we do not particullary have any large ICC’s for our variables, which indicates that there may not be a lot of simularity between our random groups.

Step 10:

Ignore all random effects from now on.

i) Apply a suitable model selection method and draw valid inferences on the response variable.

i) Apply a suitable model selection method and draw valid inferences on the response variable.

Using a Backward Elimination with Valid Post-Selection Inference to formulate a good final model to draw inferences on Income. A 60 | 20 | 20 stadnard validation split (60 for training 20 for validation 20 for testing).

set.seed(0)
Y = log(gss$Income)
X = gss[, -11]
n = dim(X)[1]
P = dim(X)[2]

train = sample(n, floor(0.6 * n))
valid = sample(setdiff(1:n, train), floor(0.2 * n))
test = setdiff(1:n, c(train, valid))
MSPE = numeric(P)

for (p in P:1) {
    fit = lm(Y ~ ., data = X[, 1:p, drop = FALSE], subset = train)

    predictions = predict(fit, newdata = X[valid, 1:p, drop = FALSE])
    MSPE[p] = mean((Y[valid] - predictions)^2)

    a = drop1(fit, test = "F")
    ind = which.max(a[-1, "Pr(>F)"])

    if (ind != p) {
        X[, c(ind, p)] = X[, c(p, ind)]
        names(X)[c(ind, p)] = names(X)[c(p, ind)]
    }
}

plot(MSPE, type = "b", pch = 16, lwd = 4, ylab = "Mean Squared\nPrediction Error")

p = which.min(MSPE)
fit = lm(Y ~ ., data = X[, 1:p, drop = FALSE], subset = c(train, valid))
summary(fit)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Y ~ ., data = X[, 1:p, drop = FALSE], subset = c(train, 
##     valid))
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -4.5632 -0.3850  0.0899  0.4116  3.9081 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                        Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)           8.603e+00  2.156e-01  39.901  < 2e-16 ***
## Income                2.968e-07  1.235e-08  24.027  < 2e-16 ***
## MaritalMarried        3.493e-01  8.943e-02   3.906 0.000104 ***
## MaritalNever Married -2.621e-01  9.772e-02  -2.682 0.007508 ** 
## MaritalSeparated     -4.443e-01  1.878e-01  -2.365 0.018310 *  
## MaritalWidowed       -1.966e-01  1.679e-01  -1.171 0.242081    
## Educ                  7.908e-02  1.209e-02   6.541 1.25e-10 ***
## SexMale               1.939e-01  6.447e-02   3.008 0.002733 ** 
## HappyPretty Happy     3.513e-01  1.145e-01   3.069 0.002239 ** 
## HappyVery Happy       4.298e-01  1.222e-01   3.517 0.000467 ***
## Hours                 6.033e-03  2.348e-03   2.569 0.010414 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8025 on 641 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.5474, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5403 
## F-statistic: 77.52 on 10 and 641 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
predictions = predict(fit, newdata = X[test, 1:p, drop = FALSE])
mean((Y[test] - predictions)^2)
## [1] 0.7165034

Step 11:

Investigate the collinearity among all quantitative covariates.

  1. Fit different types of regularized regression models with all available quantitative covariates. What can you observe about the estimated regularized regression coefficients?

  2. Apply the bootstrap method to obtain estimates for the standard errors of the estimated regularized regression coefficients. How do they compare against the estimates obtained through the ordinary least squares method?
